verses.text: The daughters of Zelophehad — Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, Tirzah — came near before Moses and the congregation: our father died in the wilderness, not in Korah's company, but in his own sin; he had no sons. Why should our father's name be done away? Give us a possession among our father's brethren. Guidance: Five sisters, five names, and the courage to stand at the tabernacle door. Their plea is neither bitter nor angry. They want their father's name remembered, his land kept. Sometimes the most consequential theology starts with women asking a fair question.

The daughters of Zelophehad — Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, Tirzah — came near before Moses and the congregation: our father died in the wilderness, not in Korah's company, but in his own sin; he had no sons. Why should our father's name be done away? Give us a possession among our father's brethren. Guidance: Five sisters, five names, and the courage to stand at the tabernacle door. Their plea is neither bitter nor angry. They want their father's name remembered, his land kept. Sometimes the most consequential theology starts with women asking a fair question.

Moses brought their cause before the LORD. The LORD: the daughters of Zelophehad speak right; thou shalt surely give them a possession of inheritance among their father's brethren; cause the inheritance of their father to pass unto them. Guidance: Moses doesn't decide it — he brings it. And God's verdict is unambiguous: they speak right. Three words rewrite a generation's inheritance law. The system bent because the daughters asked, and God said yes. Women who carry questions to God's door can move the law.

Guidance: Moses doesn't decide it — he brings it. And God's verdict is unambiguous: they speak right. Three words rewrite a generation's inheritance law. The system bent because the daughters asked, and God said yes. Women who carry questions to God's door can move the law.

The LORD: if a man die and have no son, his inheritance shall pass unto his daughter. If no daughter, to his brethren; if no brethren, to his father's brethren; if no brethren of father, to his kinsman. A statute of judgment. Guidance: One specific case becomes a general law. God turns the daughters' question into a charter that protects every fatherless family after them. The Mahlahs and Noahs and Tirzahs of a thousand generations are covered. Small obedience writes large policy in the kingdom of God.

The LORD to Moses: go up into mount Abarim, see the land I have given Israel. When thou hast seen it, thou shalt be gathered unto thy people, as Aaron — because ye rebelled at the strife of the congregation, at the water of Meribah. Guidance: A view, but no entry. God lets Moses look at the country he led forty years for — kindness inside the discipline. The leader who struck the rock will be gathered like the brother he stripped on Hor. Even saints have ceilings; even prophets pay.

Moses to the LORD: let the LORD, the God of the spirits of all flesh, set a man over the congregation, which may go out before them, lead them out and bring them in — that the congregation of the LORD be not as sheep which have no shepherd. Guidance: Moses just heard he is going to die. His first prayer is not for himself — it is for the people. Find them a shepherd; do not leave them scattered. The mark of true leadership is what you ask for after you hear bad news.

The LORD: take thee Joshua son of Nun, a man in whom is the spirit; lay thine hand upon him. Set him before Eleazar and the congregation. Put some of thine honour upon him. He shall stand before Eleazar, who shall ask counsel by the Urim. Moses did so — laid his hands upon Joshua, gave him a charge. Guidance: A spirit-filled man, publicly commissioned, with a priest to consult him and an old prophet to lay hands on him. Authority transferred not in secret but at the tabernacle door. The next chapter of Israel begins with a charge spoken aloud.
